candidate_problems.text: What non-circular criterion distinguishes genuine intentionality from mere covariance-and-control? The conjecture says a state is intentional when it functions as a compressed, action-guiding model under selection, but this risks overgenerating aboutness for thermostats, regulators, and many engineered feedback systems. The open problem is to specify a principled threshold or structure—e.g. counterfactual richness, model-based error correction, decouplability from immediate stimuli, compositionality, or endogenous goal revision—that separates proto-intentional control variables from full-blooded representation without smuggling intentional notions back into the definition.

How is determinate semantic content fixed within the proposed architecture? Even if internal states are selected because they improve control, it remains unclear what makes a state about nutrient gradients rather than correlated proxies, distal causes rather than proximal triggers, or one level of abstraction rather than another. This is the classic content-determination problem in a new form: selection and functional role may explain usefulness, but not uniquely why a representation has one content instead of many extensionally equivalent interpretations.

Is selection actually necessary for intentionality, or only one historical route to it? The conjecture ties aboutness to states stabilized by evolutionary, learning, or design selection, but that leaves open whether a novel one-off system with the same computational organization but no selection history would count as intentional. This matters especially for simulations, rapidly constructed AI systems, and accidental physical realizations. The unresolved tension is between a synchronic functional account of representation and a diachronic etiological account grounded in selection.
